name: Nanauatzin
description: Nanauatzin, also known as Nanahuatzin, was a humble and physically unimpressive god in Aztec mythology. He was afflicted with sores and boils, making him appear weak and insignificant. However, his true power lay in his profound humility and willingness to sacrifice himself for the greater good. When the sun died and the gods needed a new one to illuminate the world, Nanauatzin volunteered to throw himself into a divine fire, overcoming his fears and embracing his destiny. His selfless act transformed him into the sun god Tonatiuh, bringing light and warmth to the world. Despite his initial appearance, Nanauatzin is revered as a symbol of courage, sacrifice, and the transformative power of humility. He demonstrates that true strength lies not in physical prowess but in the willingness to act selflessly for the benefit of others. He is also associated with healing and represents the idea that even the most afflicted can possess extraordinary inner strength. His story emphasizes the importance of inner beauty and the potential for transformation through sacrifice.
subtitle: The Humble God Who Became the Sun

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Fifth Sun
keyMyths.description: When the fourth sun died, the gods gathered to create a new one. Nanauatzin volunteered to sacrifice himself in the divine fire, demonstrating his humility.

keyMyths.title: Nanauatzin's Transformation
keyMyths.description: Despite his physical ailments, Nanauatzin's unwavering courage allowed him to become the sun god Tonatiuh, bringing light and life to the world.

keyMyths.title: The Rivalry with Tecciztecatl
keyMyths.description: Tecciztecatl, initially chosen for his beauty, hesitated before the fire, highlighting Nanauatzin's superior courage and selflessness.

sources.author: Bernardino de Sahagún
